item.title: Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations 2026 – Fully Funded Youth Festival in the USA for Young Leaders Aged 18–24
item.dc:creator: Ghai A. Deng
item.description:
Young people are already leading change in their communities, classrooms, workplaces, creative industries, and entrepreneurial ventures. Yet, many national conversations about the future rarely include those who will actually shape and experience it. To address this gap, the Aspen Institute is launching Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations, the first youth-centered event in the 21-year history of […]

The post Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations 2026 – Fully Funded Youth Festival in the USA for Young Leaders Aged 18–24 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Young people are already leading change in their communities, classrooms, workplaces, creative industries, and entrepreneurial ventures. Yet, many national conversations about the future rarely include those who will actually shape and experience it.

To address this gap, the Aspen Institute is launching Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations, the first youth-centered event in the 21-year history of the Aspen Ideas Festival. Scheduled to take place from November 13–16, 2026, in Bentonville, Arkansas, this groundbreaking event is designed entirely by and for young people.

The four-day gathering will bring together 350 outstanding young adults from across the United States to engage in meaningful conversations, explore today’s most pressing issues, exchange perspectives, and collaborate on ideas that can influence the future.

One of the most attractive features of the event is that accepted participants attend completely free of charge, with travel, accommodation, meals, and all programming fully funded.

Application Deadline

Deadline: July 29, 2026

About Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations

Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations is a new initiative created by the Aspen Institute as an extension of the renowned Aspen Ideas Festival.

For more than 21 years, the Aspen Ideas Festival has served as a platform where influential thinkers, innovators, policymakers, entrepreneurs, artists, and leaders gather to discuss the world’s biggest challenges.

For the first time, this prestigious platform is creating an event that belongs entirely to young adults.

Rather than asking young people to observe conversations, Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations places them at the center of every discussion.

The event has been developed alongside the Center for Rising Generations Youth Advisory Council, ensuring that every discussion, workshop, and experience reflects issues that matter most to today’s generation.

Why Attend Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations?

Successful applicants will participate in:

  • Four days of immersive learning
  • Interactive discussions
  • Leadership conversations
  • Networking opportunities
  • Cross-cultural dialogue
  • Collaborative workshops
  • Creative experiences
  • Thought-provoking sessions
  • Community-building activities
  • Meaningful exchanges with peers from across the United States

Participants will join a diverse community representing different experiences, viewpoints, identities, and life journeys.

What the Festival Covers

Accepted participants receive full financial support, including:

  • Round-trip travel
  • Accommodation
  • Meals throughout the event
  • Full access to all festival sessions
  • Workshops
  • Interactive experiences
  • Festival programming

There is no cost for accepted participants.

Event Details

  • Event: Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations
  • Location: Crystal Bridges, Bentonville, Arkansas, USA
  • Festival Dates: November 13–16, 2026
  • Participants: 350 young people
  • Age Requirement: 18–24 years
  • Funding: Fully funded

Themes and Discussion Tracks

Participants will select and engage in one of four major tracks that reflect issues shaping the future.

1. Code + Conscious

This track explores technology and artificial intelligence from the perspective of young people already living in an increasingly digital world.

Topics include:

  • Artificial Intelligence
  • Algorithms
  • Digital platforms
  • Technology ethics
  • Future innovation
  • Responsible technology

Participants will examine how technology should serve people while engaging with perspectives that differ from their own.

2. Earn + Build

Designed for young entrepreneurs, innovators, creators, and professionals interested in economic systems.

Conversations include:

  • Entrepreneurship
  • Careers
  • Wealth creation
  • Housing
  • Education
  • Employment
  • Economic opportunity
  • Community development

Participants will exchange ideas with peers from different economic and social backgrounds.

3. Make + Move

Focused on culture as a driver of change.

Discussions include:

  • Music
  • Art
  • Storytelling
  • Creativity
  • Media
  • Identity
  • Language
  • Cultural expression

The track recognizes creativity as a powerful force for dialogue and societal transformation.

4. Spirit + Purpose

A space for exploring meaning, belief, and personal purpose.

Participants from diverse faith traditions and worldviews—including religious and non-religious perspectives—will discuss:

  • Faith
  • Values
  • Identity
  • Purpose
  • Belonging
  • Personal growth
  • Dialogue across differences

Why This Festival Matters

Young people are already:

  • Launching businesses
  • Creating artistic movements
  • Building communities
  • Organizing civic initiatives
  • Solving local challenges
  • Leading conversations about the future

Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations provides a national platform where these efforts can grow through dialogue, collaboration, and shared learning.

The festival emphasizes listening across differences and creating space for respectful conversations that generate innovative ideas.

Venue

The event will take place at Crystal Bridges in Bentonville, Arkansas.

The venue offers:

  • A world-class museum campus
  • Outdoor sculpture installations
  • Connected pavilions
  • Five miles of Ozark forest trails
  • Open-air gathering spaces
  • Natural environments designed for reflection and collaboration

Located in the heart of the country, Bentonville provides an inspiring setting for conversations that encourage fresh perspectives.

Who Should Apply?

The Aspen Institute encourages applications from young people who are already making a difference in any capacity, including:

  • Community organizers
  • Students
  • Entrepreneurs
  • Volunteers
  • Artists
  • Innovators
  • Civic leaders
  • Creators
  • Youth advocates
  • Emerging professionals

Applicants do not need:

  • A college degree
  • University enrollment
  • Prestigious credentials
  • Institutional affiliations
  • A specific career background

The selection process values authentic perspectives and demonstrated commitment over academic achievements or résumés.

Selection Process

The application process consists of three stages:

Step 1: Submit an Application

Applicants should explain:

  • Who they are
  • What issues matter to them
  • Why they want to participate

The organizers encourage honest, authentic responses rather than trying to guess what reviewers want to hear.

Step 2: Holistic Review

Applications are reviewed with the goal of assembling a diverse cohort of 350 participants representing different backgrounds, beliefs, and lived experiences.

Selection focuses on:

  • Perspective
  • Curiosity
  • Commitment
  • Diversity of experience

Rather than:

  • GPA
  • Prestige
  • Academic rankings

Step 3: Attend the Festival

Accepted participants receive complete funding covering:

  • Travel
  • Accommodation
  • Meals
  • Festival participation

Participants are expected to engage actively and respectfully throughout the event.

Eligibility Criteria

Applicants must:

  • Be between 18 and 24 years old
  • Be based in the United States
  • Submit their own application
  • Demonstrate a desire to contribute to conversations shaping the future

No specific:

  • Degree
  • Academic field
  • Professional background
  • Organization affiliation

is required.

Apply Here

Why You Should Apply

Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations is more than a conference—it is an opportunity to join a nationwide community of young leaders who are committed to creating meaningful change.

Whether your passion lies in technology, entrepreneurship, public service, the arts, civic engagement, or community leadership, this event provides an environment where ideas become action through collaboration, dialogue, and shared learning.

With all expenses covered, participants can focus entirely on learning, connecting, and contributing to conversations that will shape tomorrow.

If you are passionate about the future and eager to engage with peers from across the United States, this fully funded opportunity is one you should not miss.

For more information, visit the official website

Discover more global conference opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE

The post Aspen Ideas: Rising Generations 2026 – Fully Funded Youth Festival in the USA for Young Leaders Aged 18–24 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Youth Endowment Fund Violence Against Women and Girls Prevention Programme: Objective 2 Grant 2026 for Organizations Delivering Early Intervention in Educational Settings.
item.dc:creator: Unnah Mercy
item.description:
The Youth Endowment Fund (YEF) has opened applications for its Violence Against Women and Girls (VAWG) Prevention Programme – Objective 2, offering funding to organizations working to address inappropriate and problematic attitudes and behaviours surrounding gender norms and relationships among children and young people in educational settings across England. This funding opportunity is part of […]

The post Youth Endowment Fund Violence Against Women and Girls Prevention Programme: Objective 2 Grant 2026 for Organizations Delivering Early Intervention in Educational Settings. appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Youth Endowment Fund (YEF) has opened applications for its Violence Against Women and Girls (VAWG) Prevention Programme – Objective 2, offering funding to organizations working to address inappropriate and problematic attitudes and behaviours surrounding gender norms and relationships among children and young people in educational settings across England.

This funding opportunity is part of a broader three-year programme aimed at identifying and expanding evidence-based interventions that help prevent violence against women and girls before harmful behaviours escalate. Successful applicants will receive funding not only to deliver their intervention but also to participate in an independent evaluation that will contribute to national evidence on effective prevention strategies.

Organizations with proven interventions, strong delivery experience, and a commitment to improving young people’s understanding of respectful relationships are encouraged to apply.

About the Youth Endowment Fund Violence Against Women and Girls Prevention Programme

The Youth Endowment Fund (YEF) was established in 2019 with a £200 million endowment and a ten-year mandate from the UK Home Office to identify and support effective strategies that prevent children and young people from becoming involved in violence.

Violence against women and girls remains a significant challenge across society. Preventing harmful behaviours before they develop into serious violence requires early intervention, education, and evidence-based support systems.

This funding opportunity directly supports the UK Government’s Freedom from Violence and Abuse Strategy, published in December 2025, particularly its commitment to prevention and early intervention among young people.

Objective 2 specifically focuses on helping schools and educational settings respond effectively when children and young people begin displaying concerning attitudes and behaviours relating to gender norms and relationships.

Purpose of the Grant

The programme aims to fund targeted interventions that identify and respond to emerging inappropriate and problematic attitudes and behaviours before they escalate into serious forms of abuse or violence.

Rather than waiting until statutory services such as policing, children’s social care, or youth justice become necessary, the programme supports early intervention approaches that can positively influence young people’s attitudes and behaviours.

The overall goal is to strengthen educational settings so they are better equipped to identify, manage, and prevent harmful behaviours while promoting healthy, respectful, and equal relationships.

What the Grant Will Fund

The Youth Endowment Fund is looking for organizations delivering targeted interventions that work with children and young people aged 10–18 years.

Projects should primarily focus on supporting pupils displaying early signs of problematic attitudes and behaviours relating to gender norms and relationships.

Funded interventions may include:

  • Direct support for children and young people displaying concerning behaviours.
  • Programmes promoting healthy, respectful, and equal relationships.
  • Behaviour change interventions.
  • Early identification tools and resources.
  • Training programmes for teachers and school staff.
  • Support systems that strengthen schools’ responses to emerging concerns.
  • Educational resources that improve safeguarding practices.
  • Collaborative work between schools and community partners where there is a strong educational link.

Projects may involve community-based delivery where appropriate, provided there is a clear connection with educational institutions.

Focus on Secondary Prevention

This funding round specifically supports secondary prevention.

Secondary prevention involves identifying early warning signs and providing targeted support before behaviours become severe enough to require intervention from statutory agencies.

Examples include:

  • Addressing unhealthy gender stereotypes.
  • Challenging problematic relationship attitudes.
  • Preventing harmful behaviours from escalating.
  • Supporting young people at risk of developing abusive behaviours.
  • Building healthier relationship skills.

Evidence-Based Interventions Required

Applicants should note that the Youth Endowment Fund is not seeking entirely new programmes developed from scratch.

Instead, proposals should demonstrate that interventions are already informed by:

  • Existing research.
  • Proven theory.
  • Academic literature.
  • Established practice.
  • Previous implementation experience.
  • Evidence showing promise.

However, interventions may still require refinement or adaptation before undergoing larger-scale evaluation.

Independent Evaluation

An important feature of this grant is that every funded project will also undergo an independent evaluation.

The Youth Endowment Fund will appoint external evaluators who will work alongside funded organizations to:

  • Co-design evaluation methods.
  • Assess implementation quality.
  • Measure outcomes.
  • Generate evidence of effectiveness.
  • Inform future policy and investment decisions.

Funding is only available for projects willing to participate fully in this evaluation process.

Standalone project delivery without evaluation will not be supported.

Evaluation Scale

Most funded interventions are expected to participate in either:

  • Feasibility studies
  • Pilot evaluations

Applicants should propose:

  • Appropriate evaluation design.
  • Expected delivery scale.
  • Estimated project budget.

Final evaluation arrangements will be agreed collaboratively during the co-design phase with YEF and the appointed evaluator.

Eligibility Criteria

To qualify for funding, applicants must meet the following requirements:

Location

  • The intervention must be delivered in England.

Eligible Organizations

Applicants must be one of the following:

  • Registered charity
  • Registered company
  • Statutory body
  • Community Interest Company (CIC)

Intervention Requirements

Projects must:

  • Focus on early targeted support.
  • Address inappropriate and problematic attitudes and behaviours regarding gender norms and relationships.
  • Operate primarily within educational settings.
  • Support children and young people aged 10–18 years (or up to age 19 in SEND settings).
  • Demonstrate a clear educational link.
  • Improve knowledge, attitudes, or behaviours that contribute to preventing violence against women and girls.
  • Be sufficiently developed for evaluation.
  • Include an emerging Theory of Change.

Organizational Capacity

Applicants must demonstrate:

  • Relevant experience.
  • Strong partnerships where appropriate.
  • Ability to deliver the proposed intervention successfully.
  • Understanding of the target population and educational context.

Evaluation Commitment

Applicants must agree to:

  • Participate in independent evaluation.
  • Collaborate throughout co-design.
  • Support delivery and evaluation activities.

Click HERE to register.

Application Process

Interested organizations must submit:

  • Completed application form.
  • Completed budget template.
  • All required supporting information.

Applications should be emailed to:

grants@youthendowmentfund.org.uk

Subject line:

VAWG Prevention Programme – Objective 2

Applicants should carefully review the official application guidance before submitting their proposals.

Information Webinar

Organizations interested in applying can attend an information webinar to better understand the funding opportunity and ask questions.

Webinar Details

  • Date: Thursday, 23 July 2026
  • Time: 1:30 PM – 3:00 PM

A recording will be published on the Youth Endowment Fund website for those unable to attend.

Funding Timeline

Activity Date
Funding call opens 6 July 2026
Application deadline 9:00 AM, Wednesday, 2 September 2026
Shortlisting notification 16 September 2026
Interviews 22 September – 9 October 2026
Committee review Early November 2026
Co-design phase begins From November 2026
Delivery and evaluation Following co-design

Application Deadline

9:00 AM (BST), Wednesday, 2 September 2026

Late applications are unlikely to be considered.

To learn more about the opportunity visit HERE and Apply.

Why Organizations Should Apply

Successful applicants will benefit from:

  • Funding for intervention delivery.
  • Independent evaluation support.
  • Opportunity to strengthen evidence of programme effectiveness.
  • Contribution to national violence prevention strategies.
  • Collaboration with the Youth Endowment Fund and government partners.
  • Increased credibility through rigorous evaluation.
  • Potential to scale evidence-based interventions in the future.

Conclusion

The Youth Endowment Fund’s Violence Against Women and Girls Prevention Programme – Objective 2 represents a significant opportunity for organizations committed to preventing harmful attitudes and behaviours among children and young people. By investing in evidence-informed interventions delivered within educational settings, the programme aims to build stronger prevention systems, improve safeguarding practices, and contribute to long-term reductions in violence against women and girls. Organizations with established interventions and the capacity to participate in independent evaluation are encouraged to prepare high-quality applications before the 9:00 AM, Wednesday, 2 September 2026 deadline.

For more Grants opportunity from OFY CLICK HERE.

 

 

The post Youth Endowment Fund Violence Against Women and Girls Prevention Programme: Objective 2 Grant 2026 for Organizations Delivering Early Intervention in Educational Settings. appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026: Apply to Build AI and Machine Learning Skills Through a Real-World Challenge
item.dc:creator: Bob Waudo
item.description:
The Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026 is now accepting applications from university students and young professionals who are passionate about data science, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, and technology-driven innovation. Organized under the Hack4dev Programme, this intensive four-day regional hackathon offers participants an opportunity to strengthen their technical expertise while working […]

The post Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026: Apply to Build AI and Machine Learning Skills Through a Real-World Challenge appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026 is now accepting applications from university students and young professionals who are passionate about data science, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, and technology-driven innovation.

Organized under the Hack4dev Programme, this intensive four-day regional hackathon offers participants an opportunity to strengthen their technical expertise while working alongside like-minded innovators to solve real-world challenges using machine learning techniques and real datasets.

If you are looking to gain practical experience beyond the classroom, expand your professional network, receive mentorship from experienced experts, and become part of an international data science community, this hackathon provides an excellent platform to accelerate your journey.

About the Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP)

The Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) forms part of the broader Hack4dev Programme, an initiative dedicated to strengthening data science and artificial intelligence capacity through collaborative learning and practical problem-solving.

Unlike traditional classroom learning, Hack4dev focuses on experiential education where participants work together to address real development challenges using modern data science tools and machine learning methodologies.

During the Ghana Regional Hackathon, participants will spend four days immersed in an engaging learning environment where they will collaborate in multidisciplinary teams, analyze datasets, develop predictive models, evaluate their solutions, and present their findings before mentors and experts.

The programme is designed not only to improve participants’ technical capabilities but also to enhance teamwork, communication, creativity, and analytical thinking—skills that are increasingly sought after across industries worldwide.

Why Participate in the Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026?

Data science and artificial intelligence continue to transform industries including healthcare, agriculture, finance, education, environmental management, logistics, and public policy.

The Ghana Regional Hackathon offers participants the opportunity to gain practical exposure to these technologies while addressing meaningful challenges that have real societal impact.

Rather than working on hypothetical assignments, participants will engage with authentic datasets and practical machine learning problems that mirror challenges encountered by professionals in industry and research.

This practical experience helps bridge the gap between academic learning and workplace expectations.

What Participants Will Do During the Hackathon

Throughout the four-day programme, selected participants will work closely with mentors and fellow participants to complete various stages of a machine learning project.

Activities include:

  • Understanding the problem statement.
  • Exploring real-world datasets.
  • Cleaning and preparing data for analysis.
  • Applying machine learning techniques.
  • Developing predictive models.
  • Evaluating model performance.
  • Improving solutions through collaboration.
  • Presenting technical findings and recommendations.
  • Receiving feedback from experienced mentors.

The programme encourages interdisciplinary collaboration, allowing participants from different academic and professional backgrounds to contribute diverse perspectives toward solving complex problems.

Skills You Will Develop

Participants will strengthen both technical and professional competencies throughout the hackathon.

Technical Skills

Participants will gain practical experience in:

  • Data preparation and preprocessing.
  • Python programming for data science.
  • Machine learning model development.
  • Data analysis.
  • Model evaluation techniques.
  • Working with real datasets.
  • Artificial intelligence applications.
  • Technical presentations.

Professional Skills

Beyond technical knowledge, participants will also improve their:

  • Teamwork.
  • Leadership.
  • Communication.
  • Problem-solving.
  • Critical thinking.
  • Project management.
  • Collaboration across disciplines.
  • Time management.
  • Presentation skills.

These transferable skills are highly valuable for future academic research, graduate studies, internships, and employment opportunities.

Benefits of Participating

Successful applicants will enjoy numerous benefits throughout the programme, including:

  • Opportunity to solve a real-world machine learning challenge.
  • Practical hands-on experience in data science and artificial intelligence.
  • Guidance and mentorship from experienced trainers and professionals.
  • Collaboration with multidisciplinary teams.
  • Enhanced technical and analytical skills.
  • Improved teamwork and communication abilities.
  • Exposure to practical applications of machine learning.
  • Networking with fellow students, professionals, and experts.
  • Membership in the growing Hack4dev community.
  • Experience that can strengthen future academic and professional applications.

Who Can Apply?

Applications are open to motivated students and young professionals who meet the following eligibility requirements.

Applicants should:

  • Be currently enrolled at a university as a final-year undergraduate student or postgraduate student.
  • Or be a young professional interested in data science and AI.
  • Demonstrate interest in data science, artificial intelligence, machine learning, or related disciplines.
  • Have basic programming knowledge in Python.
  • Be enthusiastic about learning.
  • Enjoy working collaboratively within teams.
  • Be available to attend the complete four-day hackathon.
  • Be able to commute to and from the venue each day.
  • Be willing to complete all pre-hackathon preparation activities.

The organizers particularly encourage female applicants to submit applications in support of increased gender diversity within data science and artificial intelligence.

APPLY HERE

Important Participation Requirements

Applicants should carefully note the following conditions before applying:

  • Participants must provide their own laptop.
  • Attendance throughout the entire hackathon is mandatory.
  • Participants are expected to complete all assigned pre-hackathon learning materials.
  • Participants require approval from their supervisors before attending.
  • Travel expenses will not be covered.
  • Accommodation expenses will not be covered.
  • Participants must arrange their own daily transportation to and from the venue.

What You Need Before the Event

To participate successfully, applicants should have:

  • A personal laptop suitable for programming.
  • Basic Python programming knowledge.
  • Interest in data science and artificial intelligence.
  • Commitment to active participation.
  • Availability for the entire event.
  • Willingness to prepare before the hackathon begins.

Why This Hackathon Matters

As organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision-making, demand for professionals with machine learning and artificial intelligence skills continues to rise.

Participating in the DSHP Ghana Regional Hackathon enables students and young professionals to gain practical experience in solving authentic problems using modern computational techniques.

The collaborative nature of the programme also allows participants to learn from peers with different backgrounds while receiving expert guidance throughout the process.

Whether participants intend to pursue careers in technology, research, healthcare, finance, government, engineering, or entrepreneurship, the skills acquired during this hackathon can significantly strengthen their future opportunities.

Female Applicants Are Strongly Encouraged

The organizers are committed to promoting greater participation of women in science, technology, engineering, artificial intelligence, and data science.

Female students and young professionals who meet the eligibility requirements are particularly encouraged to apply and contribute their perspectives to collaborative innovation.

Application Deadline

No application deadline has been stated in the announcement. Interested applicants are encouraged to submit their applications as early as possible before registrations close.

Conclusion

The Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026 presents an outstanding opportunity for aspiring data scientists, AI enthusiasts, university students, and young professionals to develop practical skills through immersive, hands-on learning.

By working on real machine learning challenges, collaborating with multidisciplinary teams, and receiving mentorship from experienced professionals, participants will gain valuable experience that extends far beyond traditional classroom education.

If you are eager to expand your technical knowledge, strengthen your professional skills, and become part of the growing Hack4dev community, this hackathon offers an ideal environment to learn, innovate, and contribute to solving real-world challenges through data science.

For more global Competition opportunity visit OFY HERE

The post Data Science Hackathon Programme (DSHP) Ghana Regional Hackathon 2026: Apply to Build AI and Machine Learning Skills Through a Real-World Challenge appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Paid Internship in Thailand 2026: Apply for the Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) Project Team Internship for Bangkok Climate Action Week (BKKCAW 2026)
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Twumwaa
item.description:
Climate change continues to shape the future of communities, economies, and ecosystems across the globe. As countries accelerate efforts toward environmental sustainability and climate resilience, opportunities for young professionals to contribute to meaningful climate initiatives are becoming increasingly valuable. The Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) is inviting applications for its Paid Project Team Internship supporting the […]

The post Paid Internship in Thailand 2026: Apply for the Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) Project Team Internship for Bangkok Climate Action Week (BKKCAW 2026) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Climate change continues to shape the future of communities, economies, and ecosystems across the globe. As countries accelerate efforts toward environmental sustainability and climate resilience, opportunities for young professionals to contribute to meaningful climate initiatives are becoming increasingly valuable.

The Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) is inviting applications for its Paid Project Team Internship supporting the organization of Bangkok Climate Action Week (BKKCAW) 2026, one of Southeast Asia’s leading climate action events.

This internship provides recent graduates with an excellent opportunity to gain practical experience in project management, climate action programming, stakeholder coordination, venue research, and event planning while working alongside experienced professionals dedicated to building a more sustainable future.

If you are passionate about environmental sustainability, climate advocacy, collaborative problem-solving, and gaining hands-on professional experience, this internship could be an excellent stepping stone for your career.

Application Deadline: 31 July 2026

About Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI)

Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) is an organization committed to accelerating the transition toward a fair, inclusive, and climate-safe future. Through collaboration with communities, organizations, institutions, and innovators, JUTI develops initiatives that create lasting environmental and social impact.

The organization operates with three core principles:

  • Care
  • Courage
  • Collaboration

These principles guide its mission of supporting breakthrough solutions that:

  • Promote climate resilience.
  • Strengthen local communities.
  • Respect indigenous and local knowledge.
  • Restore ecosystems.
  • Build partnerships that create sustainable futures.
  • Encourage innovative climate solutions across Southeast Asia and beyond.

About Bangkok Climate Action Week (BKKCAW 2026)

As part of this internship, selected candidates will contribute directly to the planning and implementation of Bangkok Climate Action Week 2026 (BKKCAW 2026).

BKKCAW has become one of Southeast Asia’s premier climate action platforms, bringing together:

  • Climate experts
  • Policymakers
  • Researchers
  • Community leaders
  • Youth organizations
  • Civil society organizations
  • Businesses
  • Environmental advocates
  • International partners

The event focuses on encouraging collaborative climate solutions that support a greener, more inclusive, and climate-resilient future for the region.

Interns will have the opportunity to contribute to real-world projects that support this major international climate initiative.

Internship Overview

Details Information
Organization Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI)
Position Paid Project Team Intern
Number of Positions 1
Internship Type Paid Internship
Work Schedule Monday to Friday (Hybrid)
Reporting To Senior Project Coordinator
Internship Duration July to October 2026
Start Date As soon as possible
Monthly Allowance 15,000 THB
Location Bangkok, Thailand
Application Deadline 31 July 2026

What You Will Be Doing

Interns will work closely with the Project Team while supporting the planning and delivery of Bangkok Climate Action Week 2026.

The role combines research, coordination, project management, stakeholder engagement, and logistical planning.

1. Program Curation Support

Interns will help shape the content and structure of BKKCAW 2026.

Responsibilities include:

  • Researching climate-related topics and emerging trends.
  • Assisting with brainstorming sessions for conference themes.
  • Supporting the development of engaging event programmes.
  • Preparing session outlines.
  • Assisting with speaker briefing documents.
  • Helping create programme schedules.
  • Maintaining organized databases of:
    • Potential speakers
    • Community organizations
    • Government partners
    • NGOs
    • Environmental stakeholders
    • Collaborating institutions

2. Venue Research and Coordination

Another major responsibility involves identifying suitable venues throughout Bangkok for various BKKCAW activities.

Interns will:

  • Conduct venue research across Bangkok.
  • Compare venue facilities and capacities.
  • Gather quotations from venue providers.
  • Evaluate venue suitability for different programme activities.
  • Coordinate communication with venue representatives.
  • Assist with logistics planning.
  • Participate in venue inspections and site visits when necessary.

3. General Project Coordination

Interns will also assist the Project Manager with day-to-day project operations.

Responsibilities include:

  • Supporting project administration.
  • Coordinating meetings.
  • Preparing presentations.
  • Taking meeting notes.
  • Organizing documentation.
  • Updating project timelines.
  • Tracking project progress.
  • Managing spreadsheets using Microsoft Excel.
  • Collaborating with multiple internal and external partners.

Who Should Apply?

JUTI is looking for motivated graduates who are eager to develop practical experience while contributing to meaningful environmental initiatives.

Ideal candidates are individuals who:

  • Enjoy collaborative work.
  • Learn quickly.
  • Take initiative.
  • Have excellent attention to detail.
  • Can manage multiple responsibilities.
  • Are passionate about environmental sustainability.
  • Want to contribute to climate solutions that create lasting social impact.

Rather than focusing solely on academic background, JUTI values curiosity, dedication, and a genuine interest in climate action.

Eligibility Requirements

Applicants should meet the following criteria:

  • Be a recent graduate from a Bachelor’s degree programme.
  • Graduates from any academic discipline are welcome.
  • Hold Thai nationality.
  • Be eligible to work in Thailand.
  • Demonstrate a genuine interest in environmental sustainability and climate change.
  • Possess good written and spoken Thai and English language skills.
  • Be proactive and willing to learn.
  • Be hardworking and adaptable.
  • Work effectively in collaborative environments.

Required Skills

Successful applicants should possess many of the following skills:

Technical Skills

  • Microsoft Excel
  • Microsoft Office Suite
  • Google Workspace
  • Canva
  • Basic project management tools

Professional Skills

  • Research skills
  • Organization
  • Time management
  • Communication
  • Interpersonal skills
  • Stakeholder coordination
  • Attention to detail
  • Documentation
  • Administrative support

Personal Qualities

JUTI is seeking candidates who are:

  • Curious
  • Reliable
  • Responsible
  • Collaborative
  • Flexible
  • Passionate about sustainability
  • Willing to work hard
  • Motivated to develop new skills

Internship Benefits

Selected interns will receive numerous professional development opportunities, including:

  • Monthly allowance of 15,000 THB.
  • Hands-on experience working on one of Southeast Asia’s major climate events.
  • Exposure to project management and event coordination.
  • Experience working with climate professionals and community partners.
  • Practical knowledge of stakeholder engagement.
  • Opportunity to strengthen communication and organizational skills.
  • Experience in venue planning and logistics.
  • Networking opportunities within the climate and sustainability sector.
  • Professional mentoring while working alongside experienced project coordinators.
  • Valuable experience that can strengthen future career opportunities in sustainability, NGOs, international development, and project management.

Working Arrangement

Successful applicants will work:

  • Monday to Friday.
  • Hybrid working arrangement.
  • Based in Bangkok, Thailand.
  • Reporting directly to the Senior Project Coordinator.
  • Internship period running from July to October 2026.
  • Candidates are encouraged to start as soon as possible.

How to Apply

Interested applicants should prepare:

  • An updated CV.
  • A brief motivation letter explaining their interest in the internship and climate action.

Submit your applications via email to: info@juti.earth  with the subject line: Application for Paid Intern – BKKCAW 2026

Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, meaning early submission is strongly encouraged.

Important Deadline

The deadline for submitting applications is:

31 July 2026

Applicants are encouraged to submit their applications well before the closing date, as applications will be reviewed as they are received.

 Learn more about the Just Transitions Incubator(JUTI) Team Internship for Bangkok Climate Action Week

Conclusion

The Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) Paid Project Team Internship 2026 offers recent graduates an outstanding opportunity to gain practical experience while contributing to one of Southeast Asia’s most significant climate initiatives.

Beyond receiving a monthly allowance, interns will build valuable skills in project management, stakeholder engagement, programme development, venue coordination, research, and environmental advocacy. Working on Bangkok Climate Action Week 2026 provides direct exposure to collaborative climate action and allows participants to contribute to meaningful initiatives that support sustainable development across the region.

For recent graduates in Thailand who are passionate about climate change, environmental sustainability, and creating positive social impact, this internship represents an excellent opportunity to launch a career in the climate and development sector. Interested applicants should ensure they submit their CV and motivation letter before 31 July 2026 to be considered.

Discover more global Internship Opportunities on OFY HERE

The post Paid Internship in Thailand 2026: Apply for the Just Transitions Incubator (JUTI) Project Team Internship for Bangkok Climate Action Week (BKKCAW 2026) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Science Granting Councils Initiative (SGCI) STISA 2034 Multilateral Research Call 2026: US$12 Million for Africa-Led Research and Innovation
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
About the SGCI Multilateral Research Call The Science Granting Councils Initiative is a continental platform connecting national Science Granting Councils across Africa. These councils fund, manage and support the use of research and innovation to address national and regional development priorities. The current multilateral call supports the implementation of the African Union’s Science, Technology and […]

The post Science Granting Councils Initiative (SGCI) STISA 2034 Multilateral Research Call 2026: US$12 Million for Africa-Led Research and Innovation appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
About the SGCI Multilateral Research Call

The Science Granting Councils Initiative is a continental platform connecting national Science Granting Councils across Africa. These councils fund, manage and support the use of research and innovation to address national and regional development priorities.

The current multilateral call supports the implementation of the African Union’s Science, Technology and Innovation Strategy for Africa 2034, commonly known as STISA 2034. The strategy positions science, technology and innovation as essential tools for Africa’s transformation into a knowledge-based and innovation-driven continent.

STISA 2034 responds to major development challenges affecting African countries, including:

  • Climate change and environmental degradation
  • Disease outbreaks, pandemics and pressure on health systems
  • Food and nutritional insecurity
  • Limited access to affordable and reliable energy
  • Digital inequality and insufficient technological capacity
  • Economic shocks and weak regional value chains
  • Gaps between scientific research, policymaking and practical implementation

Through this funding call, SGCI intends to connect national research investments with Africa-wide priorities while encouraging institutions in different countries to combine their expertise, infrastructure, datasets and policy networks.

Objectives of the STISA 2034 Research Call

The call will support a portfolio of multi-country flagship research projects expected to achieve three major objectives.

Produce new knowledge and innovations

Projects should generate original knowledge, technologies, models, systems or practical innovations addressing development challenges identified under STISA 2034 and relevant national research priorities.

Applications must demonstrate a genuine research gap and explain how the proposed project will make a new contribution. Simply repeating an earlier study without a distinct conceptual, methodological, empirical or technological contribution is unlikely to be competitive.

Strengthen the use of research in policy and practice

Projects must include credible pathways through which research findings will reach decision-makers, communities, businesses, practitioners or other intended users.

Applicants should therefore move beyond academic publication as the primary output. Proposals should demonstrate how findings, tools or innovations can be translated into policies, programmes, services, commercial applications or improved institutional practices.

Build an integrated African science and innovation ecosystem

The call is designed to establish lasting collaboration among African research institutions. Each consortium should bring together complementary expertise from multiple participating countries and demonstrate equitable collaboration among its members.

The intention is not only to finance individual research studies but also to strengthen institutional relationships, technical capabilities, researcher mobility, shared learning and regional scientific networks.

Total Funding and Grant Size

Approximately US$12 million is expected to be distributed through the call.

Each successful consortium member may receive an award generally ranging from CAD 50,000 to CAD 300,000. However, the actual maximum available to an institution depends on the funding ceiling established for its country.

The amount is awarded separately to each participating institution, normally through that institution’s national Science Granting Council. This means that the overall consortium budget may consist of multiple country-level awards rather than one consolidated grant paid only to the lead institution.

Where a national council cannot issue a grant directly, funding may be administered by IDRC or another participating council. Funding availability within each country will remain an important factor in the final selection of successful projects.

Maximum Project Duration

Projects may run for a maximum of 36 months, including:

  • Research design and preparation
  • Data collection and fieldwork
  • Laboratory or experimental activities
  • Capacity-strengthening activities
  • Data analysis
  • Stakeholder engagement
  • Knowledge dissemination
  • Policy engagement
  • Final reporting

Applicants should propose a realistic implementation period based on the research methodology, geographic coverage and administrative complexity of the consortium.

The Five Research Streams

Every application must identify one primary thematic stream and a specific research avenue under that stream. A project may have cross-cutting elements, but its primary focus must be clearly articulated.

Stream 1: Health

The health stream seeks research that strengthens health systems, improves emergency preparedness, advances equitable healthcare and promotes interdisciplinary innovation.

Research Avenue A: Resilient health systems and primary care

Potential areas include:

  • Cost-effective and accessible healthcare models
  • Health policy and regulatory frameworks
  • Health infrastructure and information technology
  • Health workforce capacity
  • Primary healthcare systems
  • Trauma and emergency medical services
  • Healthcare access for mothers, children and adolescents
  • Health financing and public health insurance models
  • Public-private partnerships for healthcare delivery
  • Mental health, malnutrition and non-communicable diseases
  • Social and economic determinants of health

Particular attention may be given to vulnerable populations and fragile or crisis-affected contexts.

Research Avenue B: One Health preparedness and infectious disease surveillance

Research may address:

  • Integrated human, animal and environmental surveillance systems
  • Diagnostic systems and early-warning mechanisms
  • Artificial intelligence-enabled disease surveillance
  • Predictive modelling for zoonotic and climate-linked health threats
  • Pandemic preparedness
  • Emerging and re-emerging diseases
  • Transboundary disease monitoring
  • Links between health, agriculture, food security and the environment
  • Relevant Indigenous or traditional knowledge systems

Research Avenue C: Traditional knowledge and disease treatment

Potential projects may explore:

  • Strengthening and harmonising pharmacopoeia systems
  • Safety and efficacy of traditional medicines
  • Meta-analyses and guidance for traditional therapeutic products
  • Development and scaling of traditional treatment approaches
  • Biodiversity-based research
  • Bioprospecting
  • Genomics and the development of new therapeutic compounds

Health proposals should consider how their findings could support national, regional or continental health organizations and comply with applicable scientific, ethical and regulatory standards.

Stream 2: Agriculture

The agriculture stream focuses on using science, technology and innovation to transform African agrifood systems, improve food and nutritional security, increase climate resilience and strengthen value addition.

Research Avenue A: Climate-resilient and agroecological production systems

Potential areas include:

  • Climate-smart agriculture
  • Agroecological production practices
  • Sustainable farming systems
  • Soil health and soil management
  • Livestock management
  • Water management
  • Climate adaptation in rain-fed agriculture
  • Resilient irrigation systems
  • Improved agricultural productivity across different African agroecological zones

Research Avenue B: Precision agriculture, post-harvest systems and value addition

Potential projects may examine:

  • Artificial intelligence in agricultural decision-making
  • Gene-editing technologies
  • Earth observation systems
  • Drones and agricultural sensors
  • Precision input application
  • Post-harvest handling and storage
  • Food processing technologies
  • Traceability and food safety
  • Reduction of post-harvest losses
  • Agro-industrial value chains
  • Climate-smart food systems

Projects should place small-scale farmers, women and young people at the centre of the expected benefits. Innovations should also demonstrate scalability and contribute knowledge, tools or technologies that can function as public goods for the region.

Stream 3: Artificial Intelligence and Digital Technologies

This stream supports research using artificial intelligence and other digital technologies to address African development priorities, improve scientific productivity, strengthen digital skills and stimulate technology-based entrepreneurship.

Research Avenue A: AI and digital applications

Potential projects may cover:

  • AI applications for agriculture
  • AI-enabled health solutions
  • Environmental monitoring technologies
  • Digital education systems
  • AI applications for community-level challenges
  • Expansion of proven AI innovations
  • Computing infrastructure for AI research
  • Commercialisation pathways for AI-enabled products
  • Use of AI in scientific research
  • Responsible and inclusive digital systems

Research Avenue B: Learning, capacity development and entrepreneurship

Potential areas include:

  • STEM and AI skills-development models
  • Training programmes for researchers and institutions
  • Digital skills for African youth and women
  • AI fellowships and research training
  • Digital entrepreneurship
  • AI-enabled enterprise development
  • Institutional capacity for responsible AI
  • AI policy, governance and harmonisation

Applications should build on relevant national and regional initiatives wherever appropriate rather than duplicating existing platforms, systems or investments.

Stream 4: Energy

The energy stream supports research related to energy diversification, renewable technologies, energy security, storage, efficiency, productive energy use and local manufacturing.

Research Avenue A: Diversified and renewable energy systems

Possible areas include:

  • Reduction of dependence on fossil fuels
  • Energy access for marginalised populations
  • Solar energy
  • Wind energy
  • Biomass and biogas
  • Green hydrogen
  • Hydropower technologies
  • Affordable and scalable renewable energy systems
  • Traditional ecological knowledge related to clean energy
  • Energy solutions suited to diverse African settings

Research Avenue B: Energy storage, efficiency and smart systems

Projects may examine:

  • Battery technologies
  • Energy storage systems
  • Smart grids
  • Energy reliability
  • Demand-side energy efficiency
  • Clean cooking technologies
  • Off-grid energy solutions
  • Energy optimisation

Research Avenue C: Local manufacturing and commercialisation

Potential areas include:

  • Local production of energy technologies
  • Regional energy technology value chains
  • Commercialisation of energy innovations
  • Sustainable manufacturing systems
  • Energy access for women, youth and persons with disabilities
  • Industrial development
  • Energy security and strategic autonomy
  • Reduction of technology maintenance and lifecycle costs

Projects should recognise the relationship between energy and sectors such as transport, industry and agriculture, while contributing to a just and inclusive energy transition.

Stream 5: Environment

The environment stream focuses on water security, biodiversity conservation, climate adaptation, environmental governance and sustainable natural-resource management.

Research Avenue A: Water security, biodiversity and natural-resource governance

Potential projects may address:

  • Safe water and sanitation under climate stress
  • Digital and geospatial environmental technologies
  • Biodiversity conservation
  • Land and water governance
  • Sustainable natural-resource management
  • Circular-economy systems
  • Waste-to-resource technologies
  • Pollution reduction
  • Environmental impacts of mining and extractive industries

Research Avenue B: Climate resilience and adaptation

Potential areas include:

  • Climate modelling
  • Disaster-risk reduction
  • Emergency preparedness
  • Climate forecasting
  • Early-warning systems
  • Climate-resilient livelihoods
  • Ecosystem adaptation
  • Community participation in climate action
  • Scaling proven adaptation strategies

Indigenous and local knowledge systems may be incorporated where they are relevant to the research problem and methodology.

Consortium Structure

Each application must be submitted by an institutional consortium involving organizations from multiple participating SGCI countries.

The consortium must include:

  1. One lead applicant institution
  2. At least two eligible co-applicant institutions
  3. Institutions representing at least three different participating countries
  4. A principal investigator nominated by each lead or co-applicant institution
  5. No more than one lead or co-applicant institution from the same country

The lead applicant institution is responsible for submitting the single consolidated application on behalf of the consortium.

A principal investigator may submit only one application as the lead investigator but may participate as a co-applicant in other proposals. Every principal investigator must hold full-time employment within the institution they represent.

Additional organizations may participate under the responsibility of one of the funded applicant institutions. These additional participants may include:

  • Government ministries and agencies
  • Universities
  • Public research organizations
  • Private-sector organizations
  • Civil society organizations
  • Non-governmental organizations
  • Community-based organizations
  • Policy institutions
  • Technical consultants
  • Research users and implementation partners

Institutions outside the participating SGCI countries may join as additional, unfunded partners where they provide relevant technical expertise, implementation capacity or an end-user perspective. However, organizations outside Africa cannot receive research funding directly or indirectly under the call.

Eligible Institutional Applicants

Unless a country’s annex provides different requirements, lead and co-applicant institutions may generally include:

  • Public research organizations
  • Public universities
  • Private universities
  • Government research organizations

Certain countries also permit applications from startups, small and medium-sized enterprises, private-sector research organizations, legally registered associations or non-governmental organizations.

Every funded institution must:

  • Possess an independent legal identity or legal personality
  • Be legally capable of entering into contracts
  • Be authorised to receive and administer grant funding
  • Have the authority to manage the proposed research activities
  • Demonstrate sufficient financial and administrative capacity
  • Nominate a full-time principal investigator
  • Meet all additional national eligibility conditions
  • Submit a proposal directly aligned with at least one research stream and avenue

Country Funding Ceilings and Eligible Themes

The following ceilings apply to each national consortium member rather than to the consortium as a whole.

Country Eligible research streams Maximum funding per national consortium member
Botswana Agriculture, AI and Energy BWP 1,150,000
Burkina Faso AI, Agriculture and Environment XOF 50,000,000
Côte d’Ivoire Health, Agriculture and AI XOF 30,000,000
Ethiopia Health, Agriculture, AI, Energy and Environment ETB 22,500,000
Ghana Health and AI GHS 800,000
Kenya Health, Agriculture, AI, Energy and Environment KES 20,000,000
Malawi Agriculture and Energy MWK 80,000,000
Mozambique Health and Agriculture MZN 5,000,000
Namibia Agriculture, AI and Energy NAD 700,000
Nigeria AI NGN 100,000,000
Rwanda Health, Agriculture, AI, Energy and Environment RWF 100,000,000
Senegal Health, Agriculture and Environment XOF 30,000,000
Sierra Leone Agriculture and Energy SLE 1,280,000
South Africa Health, Agriculture, AI, Energy and Environment ZAR 1,500,000
Tanzania Health, Agriculture, AI, Energy and Environment TZS 300,000,000
Togo Agriculture XOF 30,000,000
Uganda Health, Agriculture, AI, Energy and Environment UGX 500,000,000
Zambia Health, Agriculture and Environment ZMW 1,000,000
Zimbabwe Agriculture, AI and Energy ZWG 1,300,000

Applicants must review the annex carefully because the country ceiling is not the only national requirement. Certain councils impose conditions relating to citizenship, residency, academic qualifications, institutional registration, gender balance, student participation, ethics approval, private-sector partnerships or eligible expenditure.

Important Country-Specific Conditions

Examples of additional requirements include:

Botswana

Principal investigators must hold at least a master’s degree or equivalent. The project team must include at least one female researcher, one early-career researcher aged 35 or below and one postgraduate student who is a citizen of Botswana. The team must also be registered in Botswana’s National Research Information Management System.

Burkina Faso

The lead institution must be based in Burkina Faso and led by Burkinabé nationals. Teams should be multidisciplinary and involve a lead institution, partner institutions and an end-user organization. Women’s representation is encouraged, and women-led proposals may receive priority in a tie.

Ethiopia

Applicants must be Ethiopian nationals or legal residents affiliated with eligible institutions such as recognised higher-education institutions, research institutes, startups, private-sector organizations or registered non-governmental organizations.

Ghana

Eligible organizations may include universities, research institutions, startups, small and medium-sized enterprises and private-sector entities. Foreign entities may participate where they demonstrate at least 30% Ghanaian ownership, management or staffing. Institutional letters of support are required.

Kenya

The principal investigator must be a Kenyan citizen affiliated with a recognised Kenyan research institution. Lead applicants must normally hold a PhD or demonstrate equivalent relevant experience. Institutional support letters are required, while the participation of women and persons with disabilities is encouraged.

Namibia

Applications must be submitted through registered research institutions holding a valid research certificate from the National Commission on Research, Science and Technology. A PhD is preferred for the principal investigator, students should be included, and teams must provide at least 30% representation of the less-represented gender.

Rwanda

The principal investigator must be a Rwandan researcher affiliated with a recognised Rwandan university or research institution. Projects must include a private-sector or industry partner in Rwanda and should demonstrate potential for employment creation and socioeconomic impact.

Sierra Leone

Principal investigators should preferably be Sierra Leonean researchers holding a PhD and relevant research experience. Projects must include early-career researchers, demonstrate appropriate research-ethics arrangements and provide institutional support.

South Africa

Principal investigators must be South African citizens or permanent residents affiliated with recognised public higher-education or research institutions. Researchers from companies, small and medium-sized enterprises and non-governmental organizations may participate but cannot serve as principal investigator. A historically disadvantaged institution must be represented in the consortium.

Tanzania

Applicants must be Tanzanian nationals or legal residents affiliated with recognised higher-education or research and development institutions. Researchers must be registered in the National Information System for Science, Technology and Innovation. Tanzania also prohibits certain expenditures, including salaries, stipends, tuition fees, institutional overheads and administrative costs.

Zambia

Eligible applicants include public and private research-performing organizations and higher-education institutions registered with Zambia’s National Science and Technology Council.

These conditions are not exhaustive. Every consortium member must examine the official country annex before finalising the partnership, budget and institutional documentation.

Eligible Project Expenses

Subject to country-specific restrictions, funding may generally cover:

  • Fieldwork and laboratory research
  • Data collection and analysis
  • Economy-class travel
  • Salaries and benefits for researchers, technicians and research assistants
  • Replacement salaries or research allowances
  • Student stipends
  • Scholarships and fellowships
  • Postdoctoral participation
  • Modest research equipment
  • Research infrastructure directly required by the project
  • Policy briefs and knowledge products
  • Academic publications
  • Stakeholder-engagement activities
  • Consultancies
  • Technical expertise
  • Monitoring, evaluation and learning
  • Subgrants
  • Indirect costs

Large equipment purchases are discouraged because of the duration and scale of the proposed projects.

Indirect costs may not exceed 13% of the overall budget, or the lower rate established by the relevant country’s funding rules.

Expression of Interest Application Requirements

The first stage of the competition requires applicants to submit an online Expression of Interest.

The application form requires the following information:

Basic project information

Applicants must provide:

  • Project title
  • Primary thematic area
  • Selected research avenue
  • Proposed project duration
  • Estimated funding requested by the lead institution
  • Estimated funding requested by each co-applicant institution

Development challenge

Applicants must provide a description of the development challenge being addressed, including:

  • National and regional context
  • Intended development outcomes
  • Relevant national, sub-regional, continental and global policies
  • The intended users of the research
  • Why the issue requires multi-country research

The maximum length is 500 words.

Research questions, relevance and novelty

Applicants must explain:

  • The main research question or questions
  • Alignment with the selected thematic stream
  • Alignment with a specific research avenue
  • Relevance to national and regional STI priorities
  • The research gap being addressed
  • How the proposed research differs from existing knowledge
  • The expected new conceptual, methodological, empirical or technological contribution

The maximum length is 500 words.

Institutional roles and capacity

Applicants must describe:

  • Each institution’s role
  • The expertise contributed by each consortium member
  • Technical capacity
  • Financial and administrative capacity
  • Ability to undertake large-scale research
  • Previous collaboration among the institutions, where applicable
  • How the partnership will operate equitably

The maximum length is 500 words.

Methodology and work plan

Applicants must provide a concise description of:

  • Proposed research methodology
  • Major project activities
  • Implementation timeline
  • Expected outputs
  • Research-capacity-building activities
  • Institutional-capacity-building activities

The maximum length is 500 words.

Documents Required at the EOI Stage

Applicants must upload:

  1. A two-page curriculum vitae for the principal investigator from every participating country
  2. A one-page curriculum vitae for up to three additional key team members across the consortium
  3. All required CVs combined into a single file
  4. A separate signed institutional letter of commitment from every lead and co-applicant organization

The letters of commitment must be issued by the institutions themselves. Listing the consortium members in the application does not replace the requirement to upload signed institutional letters.

Disclosure of Generative AI Use

Applicants must disclose whether generative artificial intelligence tools were used while preparing the application materials.

This includes tools used for:

  • Writing and editing
  • Coding
  • Design
  • Data analysis
  • Other application-development activities

Disclosure does not remove the applicant’s responsibility for the submission. The applicants remain fully accountable for the accuracy, originality, integrity and appropriateness of all submitted content.

Step-by-Step Application Process

Step 1: Review the official call and country annex

Before building the consortium, applicants should verify that each participating country is eligible for the selected research stream and that every institution meets its national conditions.

Step 2: Identify the primary research stream

Select one principal thematic stream and one corresponding research avenue. Cross-cutting projects are permitted, but the primary focus must remain explicit.

Step 3: Build a multi-country consortium

Identify a lead institution and at least two eligible co-applicant institutions from different participating SGCI countries.

The consortium should combine complementary scientific, institutional, policy and implementation capabilities.

Step 4: Use the optional matchmaking platform

Institutions that do not already have suitable partners may create a profile on the SGCI STISA 2034 matchmaking platform.

The platform allows users to:

  • Present their institutional expertise
  • Identify their preferred thematic stream
  • Find researchers and institutions in other countries
  • Search for complementary skills
  • Identify policy or end-user networks
  • Contact potential consortium partners

Use of the matchmaking platform is optional. Registration on the platform does not represent approval, endorsement or validation by the funders.

Step 5: Register on the application portal

The lead applicant should create or ACCESS AND ACCOUNT on the IDRC-managed SurveyMonkey Apply portal.

Applications may be submitted in:

  • English
  • French
  • Portuguese

Because of a technical limitation in the application system, Portuguese applicants are instructed to select Spanish from the language menu to access the Portuguese application pages. Only applications submitted in English, French or Portuguese will be accepted.

Step 6: Complete the eligibility screening

The portal will guide applicants through an initial eligibility assessment. Where the system identifies an unmet criterion, applicants may revise their information and attempt the eligibility section again before submission.

Step 7: Prepare the application offline

Applicants should download or review the application form and draft their responses in a separate saved document before entering them into the portal.

This reduces the risk of losing work and allows consortium members to review the:

  • Development challenge
  • Research questions
  • Methodology
  • Institutional roles
  • Budget estimates
  • Supporting documentation

Step 8: Upload all supporting documents

The lead institution must ensure that the combined CV file and signed letters of commitment from every applicant institution have been uploaded correctly.

Step 9: Submit one consortium application

Only the lead applicant institution should submit the application. The consortium should not submit separate applications for the same project through each participating institution.

Step 10: Confirm receipt

Applications submitted before the deadline should receive an automatic acknowledgement by email. Late or incomplete applications will not be considered.

How Expressions of Interest Will Be Reviewed

Each Expression of Interest will pass through three initial checks:

  1. Administrative review for completeness
  2. Eligibility assessment against the general and country-specific requirements
  3. Assessment of alignment with the selected thematic priority and research avenue

Applications that do not clearly demonstrate thematic alignment may be eliminated without proceeding further.

To promote balance among countries and thematic streams, no more than 40 proposals per thematic area and no more than 40 proposals involving any one country may advance to the full proposal stage.

Where the number of eligible EOIs exceeds these limits, selection will consider:

  • Alignment of institutional and individual expertise with the proposed research
  • Strength of the research topic
  • Relevance to national and regional priorities
  • Importance to intended end users
  • Capacity of consortium institutions
  • Quality and complementarity of the partnership

Full Proposal Stage

Only applicants with successful Expressions of Interest will be invited to submit a full proposal.

The full proposal is expected to include:

  • A detailed development context and problem statement
  • Expanded research questions
  • Evidence of novelty
  • Institutional eligibility and contributions
  • A detailed budget
  • Research methodology
  • Month-by-month project timeline
  • Theory of change or outcome pathway
  • Researcher and institutional capacity strengthening
  • Benefits for students and early-career researchers
  • Gender, equity and inclusion measures
  • Research risks and mitigation
  • Research ethics and safeguarding arrangements
  • Monitoring plans and indicators
  • Knowledge-sharing strategy
  • Policy engagement plan
  • Detailed CVs
  • Up to three letters of support from stakeholders, collaborating institutions or intended research users

The proposal should clearly demonstrate how intended users have informed the project and how results will be positioned for practical use.

Full Proposal Evaluation Criteria

Full proposals will be reviewed by an independent peer-review committee.

High-quality research: 65%

This component considers:

  • Scientific rigour
  • Research legitimacy
  • Relevance to African STI priorities
  • Equality, diversity and inclusion
  • Research importance
  • Originality and novelty
  • Engagement of intended users
  • Knowledge-sharing plans
  • Feasibility
  • Ethical and safe implementation

Team composition and institutional strength: 25%

This component assesses:

  • Qualifications of principal investigators
  • Technical expertise
  • Institutional capacity
  • History of collaboration
  • Complementarity of expertise
  • Equitable partnership arrangements
  • Inclusivity of the team

Capacity strengthening: 10%

This component examines how the project will strengthen:

  • Research institutions
  • Graduate students
  • Early-career researchers
  • Postdoctoral researchers
  • Civil society organizations
  • Research users
  • Community members

Projects must achieve a total score of at least 75% to enter the group of fundable proposals. Reaching this threshold does not guarantee an award because final decisions will also depend on available country funding and the need for geographic and thematic balance.

Gender Equality, Diversity and Inclusion

All projects are expected to integrate equality, diversity and inclusion throughout the research cycle.

Applications should explain:

  • How women and underrepresented groups will participate in the research team
  • How vulnerable populations will influence the research design
  • How intended beneficiaries will be engaged
  • How gender and other inequalities affect the research problem
  • Whether sex-disaggregated or gender-disaggregated data will be collected
  • How inclusion will be reflected in project indicators
  • How the knowledge-translation plan will consider different groups
  • Which team members possess relevant gender and inclusion expertise
  • Whether sufficient funding has been allocated for inclusion-related activities

The call recognises that inequality may result from interacting factors such as gender, age, disability, race, ethnicity, citizenship, income, social class, religion and geographic location. Proposals should therefore use an intersectional approach wherever relevant.

Important Dates

  • Application portal opened: July 3, 2026, at 09:00 EDT
  • First information webinar: July 23, 2026, from 8:00 a.m. to 10:00 a.m. EDT
  • Second information webinar: August 13, 2026, from 8:00 a.m. to 10:00 a.m. EDT
  • Expression of Interest deadline: September 25, 2026, at 23:59 EDT
  • Eligibility screening and country review: October 9, 2026
  • Shortlisted applicants informed: October 19, 2026
  • Unsuccessful EOI applicants notified: November 2, 2026
  • Webinar for full proposal applicants: November 5, 2026
  • Full proposal deadline: December 4, 2026
  • Successful applicants informed: February 15, 2027
  • Unsuccessful full-proposal applicants notified: March 15, 2027
  • Expected award or approval of proposals: May 3, 2027

Both initial webinars are expected to cover the same content. Recordings are expected to be published after the sessions, while the official FAQ may be updated based on questions raised by applicants.

Application Deadline

The deadline for submitting an Expression of Interest is:

September 25, 2026, at 23:59 Eastern Daylight Time, UTC-4.

Applications received after the deadline will not be considered.

Applicants should avoid waiting until the final hours because the application requires coordination among several institutions, multiple institutional signatures, combined CV documentation and country-specific eligibility verification.

Important Clarifications Applicants Should Obtain

Applicants should be aware that some official materials currently contain inconsistent instructions.

Maximum consortium size

The detailed call document and application portal state that a consortium may include one lead institution and as many as five co-applicant institutions. However, the official FAQ and IDRC funding summary describe a maximum of four co-applicants, producing a maximum consortium size of five institutions.

Applicants planning to include more than four co-applicants should obtain written clarification from IDRC before finalising the consortium.

Currency for the EOI budget

The detailed call states that the total EOI budget should be presented in the local currency of the lead applicant institution, while the FAQ recommends preparing EOI budgets in Canadian dollars.

Applicants should follow the instructions displayed in the live application form and seek clarification where the portal does not resolve the difference.

Questions concerning the call may be submitted to SGCSSA@idrc.ca. Inquiries should be sent at least 36 hours before the application deadline where a response is required before submission.

Conclusion

The SGCI STISA 2034 Multilateral Research Call represents a significant opportunity for African research institutions to secure funding for ambitious, multi-country projects addressing the continent’s most urgent development priorities.

With approximately US$12 million available across health, agriculture, artificial intelligence, energy and environmental research, the call is designed to support projects that combine scientific quality with practical policy relevance, institutional capacity strengthening and measurable benefits for African communities.

Competitive applications will require more than a strong research idea. They must establish an eligible and complementary consortium, demonstrate clear novelty, align with both national and continental priorities, engage intended users, integrate equality and inclusion, and present a credible pathway from research evidence to policy or practical application.

Institutions considering an application should begin consortium development and document preparation well before the September 25, 2026 deadline. They should also review every relevant country annex, verify national funding limits and resolve any uncertainty concerning consortium size or budget currency directly with the call administrators before submitting the Expression of Interest.

For more global opportunities, visit HERE.

The post Science Granting Councils Initiative (SGCI) STISA 2034 Multilateral Research Call 2026: US$12 Million for Africa-Led Research and Innovation appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: ACU Global Grants 2026: Up to GBP 5,000 for Undergraduate Students to Attend International Summer and Winter Schools in Commonwealth Countries
item.dc:creator: Ning Ma
item.description:
The Association of Commonwealth Universities (ACU) has officially opened applications for the ACU Global Grants 2026, providing undergraduate students at ACU member universities with funding to participate in short-term international academic programmes across the Commonwealth. The Global Grants are designed to encourage academic development, cross-cultural learning, and international collaboration by enabling students to attend summer […]

The post ACU Global Grants 2026: Up to GBP 5,000 for Undergraduate Students to Attend International Summer and Winter Schools in Commonwealth Countries appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Association of Commonwealth Universities (ACU) has officially opened applications for the ACU Global Grants 2026, providing undergraduate students at ACU member universities with funding to participate in short-term international academic programmes across the Commonwealth.

The Global Grants are designed to encourage academic development, cross-cultural learning, and international collaboration by enabling students to attend summer schools or winter schools hosted by another ACU member institution in a different Commonwealth country.

Successful applicants can receive up to GBP 5,000 to support their participation in eligible academic programmes. The grants also help students build international networks, gain global academic exposure, and broaden their educational experiences beyond their home institutions.

Applications are open from 14 July 2026 and will close on 25 August 2026 at 15:00 UTC.
